title: The Practice of Tolerating Good Things
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: For trauma survivors, goodness can feel threatening. Learn why your nervous system resists joy and how to gently expand your capacity to tolerate the light.
imageDescription: A piece of beautifully repaired kintsugi pottery sits on a rustic wooden table, bathed in the warm, golden light of early morning. The intricate gold seams glow, symbolizing the beauty and strength that can be found in healing after being broken. The scene is serene, luminous, and evokes a sense of gentle, earned peace.
finalH2: The Courage to Remain in the Light
htmlContent:
What happens when the body, long conditioned for the impact of the fall, is suddenly asked to tolerate the feeling of being held? For those of us who have navigated the intricate aftermath of complex trauma, the arrival of something genuinely good—a loving partner, a moment of profound peace, a long-sought professional success—can feel more dysregulating than the chaos it replaced. The system, calibrated for threat, meets kindness with suspicion, and ease with a bracing tension, a deep and often unconscious preparation for the other shoe that has always, eventually, dropped. We find ourselves waiting for the familiar storm in the quiet of a sunny afternoon, a testament to the profound loyalty of a nervous system that learned survival first and has yet to learn safety. This is not a cognitive error or a failure of gratitude; it is the predictable echo of a history written in the body, a body that must now undertake the slow, deliberate practice of learning to tolerate the light.

The Architecture of Bracing

Our entire physiology is shaped by experience, a truth that becomes profoundly evident when we examine the legacy of developmental trauma. The constant, low-grade activation of the sympathetic nervous system, the hyper-vigilant scanning of our environment for cues of danger, the subtle and not-so-subtle ways we hold our breath—these are not choices but adaptations. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals describe this phenomenon as a kind of internal armor, welded into place so long ago that its weight feels like a part of their own skeleton. When a genuine moment of safety or connection appears, the armor doesn’t simply dissolve; its very presence becomes more pronounced, a rigid container for a softness it was never designed to hold. The body braces for an impact that isn’t coming, and in doing so, it inadvertently pushes away the very connection it craves. We are left with the bewildering experience of wanting to let the good in while our entire neurobiological framework is screaming to keep it out. This is the deep, paradoxical challenge of learning to receive after a lifetime of learning to deflect.
The question is never whether the pain will come. The question is whether you'll meet it with presence or with narrative.

Neuroception and the Bias for Threat

The work of researcher Stephen Porges provides a crucial map for understanding this territory, particularly through his concept of neuroception. Unlike perception, which is a cognitive process, neuroception is the nervous system’s instantaneous, unconscious scanning of the environment for cues of safety, danger, and life-threat. For a system conditioned by complex trauma, this scanning mechanism develops a profound and persistent bias for threat. It is a system that has learned, through relentless experience, that it is far less costly to mistake a friend for a foe than to mistake a foe for a friend. The result is a neuroceptive process that is exquisitely tuned to find the subtle hint of danger in an otherwise safe environment—the slight shift in a partner’s tone, the un-returned email, the quiet moment that feels too quiet. This isn’t pessimism; it’s a survival strategy that has outlived its usefulness. The work, then, is not to convince the thinking mind that things are safe, but to offer the nervous system new data, to gently and repeatedly expose it to experiences of safety and connection that it can begin to trust on its own terms. It is a slow recalibration, a process of updating the body’s deep, implicit archives of what is possible in the world, including the possibility of genuine, sustainable goodness.

The Upper Limit Problem in Trauma Recovery

We often talk about the fear of failure, but for many survivors of complex trauma, the fear of success—or more accurately, the fear of goodness—is a far more potent and confusing force. The experience of finally achieving a level of peace, connection, or stability can trigger an internal alarm system, a phenomenon sometimes referred to as an "upper limit problem." It’s as if we have an internal thermostat for how much joy, love, or ease we are allowed to experience, and when we exceed that setting, we unconsciously engage in behaviors that bring us back down to a more familiar, albeit painful, temperature. This can sign as picking a fight in a healthy relationship, procrastinating on a project that is going well, or finding our bodies wracked with anxiety in the midst of a peaceful vacation. It is a heartbreaking form of self-sabotage, but it is not born of a desire to suffer. It is born of a deep, somatic loyalty to a state of being that, for a very long time, was synonymous with survival. Expanding our capacity for joy requires that we become aware of this upper limit, that we notice the subtle ways we begin to contract when life starts to feel expansive. It is a practice of staying present with the discomfort of the unfamiliar, of allowing the thermostat to be reset, one tender, terrifying degree at a time. This is a core tenet of rebuilding the self after trauma.
The breath doesn't need your management. It needs your companionship.

The Guilt of Goodness and the Shame of Deserving

Beneath the physiological bracing and the upper limits often lies a more insidious layer of difficulty: the profound sense of guilt and shame that can accompany positive experiences. For those who grew up in environments where their worth was constantly in question, or where love was transactional and conditional, the experience of unconditional positive regard can feel deeply undeserved. A client once described this to me as feeling like an imposter in her own life, as if the happiness she was experiencing was a borrowed coat that would soon be reclaimed. This feeling is often compounded by a kind of survivor’s guilt, a sense that it is a betrayal of one’s past suffering, or of family members still mired in dysfunction, to feel genuine joy. The mind generates narratives to make sense of this discomfort, whispering that we are not good enough, that we are fooling everyone, that it is only a matter of time before we are found out. The practice here is one of radical self-compassion, of acknowledging the part of us that feels unworthy without letting it drive the bus. It is the work of gently separating the feeling of joy from the story of what that joy means, of allowing ourselves to have the experience without the crushing weight of a narrative that says we don’t deserve it. We can learn more about this from a wealth of psychological resources.

The Deliberate Practice of Tolerating Light

So how do we begin to expand our capacity for goodness? It is not a passive process, but a deliberate and courageous practice of titration and presence. It begins with the smallest moments. It might be the practice of staying with the warmth of the sun on your skin for three full breaths before the mind declares it boring or dangerous. It might be the practice of truly receiving a compliment, of letting it land in the body before the impulse to deflect it arises. It is the practice of noticing the impulse to brace for the worst and, instead, consciously softening the muscles in the jaw, the shoulders, the belly. A client once found it helpful to practice what she called "somatic snapshots," intentionally pausing in a moment of ease—the taste of her morning coffee, the sound of her cat purring—and consciously taking in the sensory details, letting her body know, "This is also real. This is also happening." It is a process of building a new kind of evidence, of creating a portfolio of moments that can begin to counterbalance the weight of a lifetime of difficult ones. It is not about forcing gratitude or pretending that the difficult is not also present. It is about making room for both, for the complexity of a life that holds both the echo of trauma and the emergent possibility of something new. This is the essence of somatic healing.
What if the restlessness isn't a problem to solve but a signal to follow?

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel more anxious when things are going well?

This is a common experience for those with a history of complex trauma. Your nervous system became highly adapted to detecting and responding to threats. When things are calm and good, this hyper-vigilant system can interpret the lack of threat as a sign that you're missing something, leading to a spike in anxiety as it searches for the danger it expects to find. It's a sign that your body's survival mechanism is working perfectly, just in a context that no longer requires it.

How can I practice tolerating joy without feeling overwhelmed?

The key is to start small, a practice known as titration. Instead of trying to immerse yourself in overwhelming joy, focus on tiny, manageable moments of goodness. This could be savoring a single bite of food, noticing the feeling of a soft blanket for a few seconds, or allowing yourself to feel a flicker of pride in a small accomplishment. By practicing with these micro-moments, you gradually and gently expand your nervous system's capacity to hold positive experiences without it feeling like too much, too soon. You can also explore resources on trauma-informed care from organizations like the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation or the National Institute of Mental Health.

The Courage to Remain in the Light

Ultimately, the practice of tolerating good things is an act of profound courage. It is the courage to disappoint the part of us that is still waiting for the other shoe to drop, the part that believes its vigilance is the only thing keeping us safe. It is the courage to feel the vulnerability of an open heart, to stand in the quiet clearing of a peaceful moment and not run back to the familiar forest of our own anxieties. It is the willingness to be changed by joy, to be remade by love, to be redefined by peace. This is not a destination we arrive at, but a practice we return to, moment by moment. It is the slow, steady, and often fumbling process of teaching an old dog new tricks, of showing the loyal soldier of our nervous system that the war is, in fact, over. The path to a regulated and fulfilling life is not about eliminating challenges, but about building the capacity to experience goodness without apology or fear. It is about learning to trust the ground of our own being, even when that ground is unfamiliar. And it is about discovering, in our own time and in our own way, that we are capable of holding not just the weight of our past, but the light of our present. The real question is not whether you deserve to feel good, but whether you are willing to do the work of learning how.

For those on a path of deep healing and self-discovery, exploring the nature of consciousness can be a powerful adjunct to trauma work. You can learn more about this at Kalesh.love.
